skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Browser automation for AI agents via inference.sh. Navigate web pages, interact with elements using @e refs, take screenshots, record video. Capabilities: web scraping, form filling, clicking, typing, drag-drop, file upload, JavaScript execution. Use for: web automation, data extraction, testing, agent browsing, research.
skill.summary: Browser automation skill that enables AI agents to navigate and interact with web pages through a simple element reference system.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: All 609 static analysis findings are false positives from documentation and template files. The skill contains only legitimate bash scripts demonstrating browser automation workflows. External command patterns are CLI examples showing proper infsh usage. Network findings are example URLs in documentation. No malicious code, credential exfiltration, or unauthorized data transmission detected. The skill is a well-documented browser automation tool with no security concerns.

content.user_title: Automate Web Browsing with AI Agents
content.value_statement: This skill enables AI agents to navigate and interact with web pages automatically. It provides a simple element reference system for clicking links, filling forms, and extracting data from any website.
actual_capabilities: Navigate to any URL and retrieve page content with element references
Click buttons, links, and interactive elements using @e reference system
Fill text inputs, select dropdowns, check boxes, and upload files
Take screenshots of viewport or full pages for debugging
Record browser sessions as video for documentation
Execute custom JavaScript code on web pages
Manage persistent browser sessions for authenticated workflows
Configure proxy settings for geo-location testing
Drag and drop elements for complex interactions
limitations: Element references are invalidated after page navigation and require re-snapshot
Cannot interact with elements that are hidden or outside viewport
Does not support browser extensions or custom profiles
Requires the inference.sh CLI tool to be installed and authenticated
Session timeouts may occur with long-running automation tasks
Dynamic content loaded after initial page load may require explicit waits

use_cases.title: Automated Form Testing
use_cases.description: QA testers can fill and submit web forms repeatedly to validate functionality and catch regression bugs.

use_cases.title: Data Extraction from Websites
use_cases.description: Researchers can navigate to data sources and extract structured information from multiple pages using element references.

use_cases.title: Authenticated Workflow Automation
use_cases.description: Marketing teams can automate repetitive tasks in web applications that require login, such as content publishing or lead management.

prompt_templates.title: Navigate and Extract
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to https://example.com, take a screenshot, and extract all links from the page using @e refs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic web scraping to collect URLs from a landing page

prompt_templates.title: Form Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Open https://example.com/contact, identify the form fields, fill in name as 'John Doe' and email as 'john@example.com', then submit the form and verify success.
prompt_templates.scenario: Automating contact form submission with validation

prompt_templates.title: Authenticated Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Login to https://app.example.com with credentials from environment, navigate to dashboard, take a screenshot, and extract the user profile data.
prompt_templates.scenario: Accessing authenticated pages to retrieve user-specific data

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Step Workflow with Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a video recording session, navigate through the checkout process on https://shop.example.com (add item, view cart, checkout), close session to save the video.
prompt_templates.scenario: Documenting a multi-step user journey for demonstration

output_examples.input: Navigate to https://example.com and list all interactive elements
output_examples.output: Successfully navigated to https://example.com. Found 5 interactive elements: @e1 [a] 'More information', @e2 [input] placeholder='Search', @e3 [button] 'Submit', @e4 [select] 'Choose option', @e5 [checkbox] name='agree'. Screenshot saved.

output_examples.input: Fill the login form with test credentials and submit
output_examples.output: Filled username field (@e1) with 'testuser@example.com' and password field (@e2) with '********'. Clicked submit button (@e3). Waiting 3 seconds for redirect. Current URL: https://example.com/dashboard. Login successful.

output_examples.input: Extract all article titles from the blog page
output_examples.output: Executed JavaScript to query article titles. Found 12 articles: 'Introduction to Browser Automation', '10 Tips for Web Scraping', 'Understanding Element References', 'Advanced Playwright Techniques', and 8 more.

best_practices: Always re-snapshot after navigation or DOM changes to get fresh element references
Use the --session flag to maintain browser state across multiple commands
Implement cleanup handlers in scripts to ensure sessions close properly
Start with discovery mode to identify element refs before writing automation
anti_patterns: Do not use stale @e refs after page navigation as they will no longer match elements
Avoid hardcoding credentials in scripts - use environment variables instead
Do not skip snapshot calls after dynamic content loads or form submissions
Never store session IDs in version control as they contain temporary authentication tokens

faq.question: What does the @e ref system mean?
faq.answer: The @e ref system provides unique identifiers for each interactive element on a page. For example, @e1 might refer to the first button, @e2 to the first input field. These refs make it easy to reference elements without complex CSS selectors.

faq.question: Why do my element references stop working?
faq.answer: Element references are invalidated whenever the page navigates or the DOM changes significantly. Always call the snapshot function after navigation to get fresh references for the new page state.

faq.question: Can I use this skill to scrape JavaScript-heavy websites?
faq.answer: Yes, the underlying Playwright engine fully executes JavaScript, so dynamic content loaded via client-side rendering will be accessible. Use the 'wait' action to allow time for content to load.

faq.question: How do I handle login forms and authentication?
faq.answer: Use the authenticated session template pattern: navigate to login, fill credentials with the fill action, click submit, wait for redirect, then continue with authenticated actions. Session state persists across commands.

faq.question: What happens if an element is not found?
faq.answer: If an @e ref no longer exists on the page, the interact command will fail with an error. Always snapshot after navigation changes and verify element refs before attempting interactions.

faq.question: Can I run multiple browser sessions in parallel?
faq.answer: Yes, each session has a unique session_id. You can start multiple sessions with --session new and manage them independently by passing the correct session_id to each command.
